original_abstract:
Large language model (LLM) systems, such as ChatGPT and Gemini, exhibit remarkable reasoning and question-answering abilities but are prone to "hallucinations"—generating false or unsubstantiated information. This unreliability remains a major barrier to their adoption in critical fields where misinformation can have severe consequences, ranging from the fabrication of legal precedents to life-threatening errors in medical fields like radiology. While techniques like human supervision and reinforcement learning have been used to encourage truthfulness, they have only been partially successfull in curbing these errors.

In this work, we present a general, statistically grounded method for detecting a specific subset of hallucinations known as "confabulations"—arbitrary and incorrect generations that occur when a model lacks sufficient knowledge. Our approach, termed semantic entropy, addresses the fundamental challenge that a single idea can be expressed through many different word sequences. By computing uncertainty at the level of meaning rather than specific tokens, we can effectively distinguish between lexical variation and genuine model confusion.

Our method is entirely unsupervised and generalizes across various datasets and tasks without requiring task-specific data or a priori knowledge. We demonstrate its effectiveness across multiple model families and scales, including LLaMA, Falcon, and Mistral, as well as in complex, paragraph-length generations like biographies using GPT-4. By accurately identifying when a prompt is likely to trigger a confabulation, our method provides users with a reliable signal of when to exercise caution, opening new pathways for deploying LLMs in high-stakes environments where reliability is paramount.
humanized_abstract:
Everyone is abuzz with the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT and Gemini, which can potentially act as highly advanced “answer machines”. These systems are capable of providing very plausible reasoning and answers for many questions. The downsides are that they can provide answers that are completely made up, without referring to any underlying knowledge or doing any research. While sometimes entertaining, this propensity for hallucination means that these systems are currently not fit for serious use, especially in situations where misinformation can be potentially dangerous – e.g., when generating court decisions from scratch (and therefore potentially creating legal precedent out of thin air), or when diagnosing ailments from medical images (a radiology application where a false positive or negative can potentially mean the difference between life and death). Currently, the only attempts to curtail this aspect of LLMs have come in the form of manually trained datasets and reinforcement learning techniques to try to enforce more factual outcomes. And while some success has been achieved, it is only minor.

We introduce a general, probabilistic framework for detecting a particular class of hallucinations known as confabulations, defined as the production of completely arbitrary and false information that is produced when a model realises that it has insufficient knowledge to generate an appropriate response. We name our metric semantic entropy. The problem that our method is designed to address is that there are many possible grammatical ways to express the same idea. Most metrics measure the uncertainty in the tokenisation of a response rather than at the semantic level. This results in measuring noise due to the many possible ways to express an idea rather than actual model uncertainty.

Our method is fully unsupervised and naturally generalizes to new benchmarks and applications regardless of the specific dataset or application. We apply it to multiple model families and scales of language models, including LLaMA, Falcon and Mistral as well as to more nuanced and long form sequences like the generation of a full biography using GPT-4. Importantly our method serves as a very clear indicator as to when a user is about to ask a confabulation-triggering question giving the opportunity to approach with greater care and providing an important tool to increase the robustness and safety of Large Language Models for use in high risk situations.

document.input:
Large language model (LLM) systems, such as ChatGPT and Gemini, exhibit remarkable reasoning and question-answering abilities but are prone to "hallucinations"—generating false or unsubstantiated information. This unreliability remains a major barrier to their adoption in critical fields where misinformation can have severe consequences, ranging from the fabrication of legal precedents to life-threatening errors in medical fields like radiology. In this work, we present a general, statistically grounded method for detecting a specific subset of hallucinations known as "confabulations"—arbitrary and incorrect generations that occur when a model lacks sufficient knowledge. Our approach, termed semantic entropy, addresses the fundamental challenge that a single idea can be expressed through many different word sequences. By computing uncertainty at the level of meaning rather than specific tokens, we can effectively distinguish between lexical variation and genuine model confusion.

By accurately identifying when a prompt is likely to trigger a confabulation, our method provides users with a reliable signal of when to exercise caution, opening new pathways for deploying LLMs in high-stakes environments where reliability is paramount.
